before: Abdullah Ibrahim, previously known as Dollar Brand, is a South African pianist and composer. His music reflects many of the musical influences of his childhood in the multicultural port areas of Cape Town, ranging from traditional African songs to the gospel of the AME Church and Ragas, to more modern jazz and other Western styles. Ibrahim is considered the leading figure in the subgenre of Cape jazz. Within jazz, his music particularly reflects the influence of Thelonious Monk and Duke Ellington. He is known especially for "Mannenberg", a jazz piece that became a notable anti-apartheid anthem. The most important South African jazz recording. 'Mannenberg' became a rallying cry for freedom, played at protests and celebrations.
after: Mannenberg - Is Where It's Happening is a 1974 album by South African pianist Abdullah Ibrahim, released on the Sun label. The title track "Mannenberg" is its centerpiece. Other tracks include "Soweto Is Where It's At" and "The Pilgrim".
evidenceUsed: ours fields only (title, artist, year, label, keyTracks); no entity-matched MusicBrainz release and the stored wiki is the artist page (wikipedia=no)

before: Monk's Dream is an album by jazz pianist Thelonious Monk, released by Columbia Records in January 1963. It was Monk's first album for Columbia following his five-year recording period with Riverside Records.
after: Monk's Blues is a 1969 album by pianist Thelonious Monk, issued on CBS. The record sets Monk compositions and standards in a large-ensemble setting. Tracks include "Let's Cool One", "Reflections", "Brilliant Corners" and "Straight No Chaser".

before: Jazz fusion is a popular music genre that developed in the late 1960s when musicians combined jazz harmony and improvisation with rock music, funk, and rhythm and blues. Electric guitars and basses, amplifiers, and keyboard instruments that were popular in rock began to be used by jazz musicians, particularly those who had grown up listening to rock and roll.
after: Morning with Swing Jazz: Guitar, Trumpet, Saxophone & More, Melodies of Piano, Instrumental Smooth Jazz Music is a 2019 album credited to The Jazz Messengers and Piano Dreamers, released on Diamond Music Record. It is an instrumental smooth-jazz collection. Tracks include "The Best of Vintage Jazz", "Romance Time After Time" and "Love Serenade".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title, date 2019-09-21, artists The Jazz Messengers + Piano Dreamers, label Diamond Music Record, tracklist

before: Eugene McDuffy, known professionally as "Brother" Jack McDuff or "Captain" Jack McDuff, was an American jazz organist and organ trio bandleader. He was most prominent during the hard bop and soul jazz era of the 1960s, often performing with an organ trio. He is also credited with giving guitarist George Benson his first break.
after: On With It! is an album by organist Brother Jack McDuff, released on Prestige (PRST 7851) in 1971. Tracks include "Hey Lawdy Mama", "Drown in My Own Tears" and "Groanin'".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title On With It!, artist Brother Jack McDuff, date 1971, label Prestige PRST 7851, tracklist

before: Night Train is an album by the Oscar Peterson Trio, released in 1963 by Verve Records. The album includes jazz, blues and R&B standards, as well as "Hymn to Freedom", one of Peterson's best known original compositions. As a virtuoso who is considered to be one of the greatest jazz pianists of all time, Peterson released more than 200 recordings, won eight Grammy Awards, as well as a lifetime achievement award from the Recording Academy, and received numerous other .
after: Oscar Peterson Plays George Gershwin is a 1953 album by pianist Oscar Peterson on Clef Records. It collects Peterson's readings of Gershwin material, including "The Man I Love", "It Ain't Necessarily So", "Strike Up the Band!" and "A Foggy Day".

before: "Pink Elephants on Parade" is a song and scene from the 1941 Disney animated feature film Dumbo in which Dumbo and Timothy Q. Mouse, having accidentally become intoxicated, see pink elephants sing, dance, and play musical instruments during a hallucination sequence. After the sequence, Dumbo and Timothy wake up, hungover, in a tree. It is at this point that they realize that Dumbo can fly.
after: Pink Elephants on Parade is a 2024 album credited to Sun Ra and his Arkestra. It collects Arkestra performances of Disney songs, including "Someday My Prince Will Come", "Zip-A-Dee-Doo-Dah", "Let's Go Fly a Kite" and the title track "Pink Elephants on Parade".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title Pink Elephants on Parade, artists Sun Ra + Arkestra, date 2024-07-05, tracklist of Disney songs

before: Charles Mingus in Paris: The Complete America Session is a compilation of two albums by American bassist, composer and bandleader Charles Mingus which were recorded in Paris in 1970 and first released on the French America label as Blue Bird and Pithycanthropus Erectus. The compilation was released on the Sunnyside label in 2006 with all the released tracks combined on one CD and a second CD of additional tracks featuring false starts and incomplete takes from the session.
after: Pithecanthropus Erectus + The Clown is a 2009 compilation of bassist Charles Mingus, released on Essential Jazz Classics (EJC55437). It pairs the albums Pithecanthropus Erectus and The Clown on a single disc. Tracks include "Pithecanthropus Erectus", "Haitian Fight Song", "Reincarnation of a Lovebird" and "The Clown".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title Pithecanthropus Erectus + The Clown, artist Charles Mingus, date 2009, label Essential Jazz Classics EJC55437, secondaryType Compilation, tracklist

before: Joseph Arthurlin Harriott was a Jamaican jazz musician and composer, whose principal instrument was the alto saxophone. According to George McKay in Circular Breathing: The Cultural Politics of Jazz in Britain, Harriott was 'responsible for a series of brilliant experiments in new music in Britain through the 1960s'. His work was 'crucial' in two areas of innovation: free music and then global music fusion.
after: Poetry and Jazz in Concert (Record One) is a 1964 British album on Argo (ZDA 26). It documents a poetry-and-jazz concert, pairing poets Adrian Mitchell and Dannie Abse with The Michael Garrick Quintet and saxophonist Joe Harriott alongside Shake Keane.
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title, date 1964, country GB, label Argo ZDA 26, credited artists Adrian Mitchell, Dannie Abse, The Michael Garrick Quintet, Joe Harriott, Shake Keane

before: The Quintette du Hot Club de France, often abbreviated "QdHCdF" or "QHCF", was a jazz group founded in France in 1934 by the guitarist Django Reinhardt and the violinist Stéphane Grappelli. It was active in one form or another until 1948.
after: Quintette du Hot Club de France is a compilation credited to guitarist Django Reinhardt, released on the Rockin' Chair label. It gathers gypsy-jazz sides, including "Exactly Like You", "Rose Room", "Body and Soul", "Limehouse Blues" and "Djangology".

before: Goodbye is an album by vibraphonist Milt Jackson recorded in 1973 and released on the CTI label. Musician player Milt Jackson's big band sensibility is on display in this 1965 session, a document of the hard bop era.
after: Ray Brown / Milt Jackson is a 1965 album co-led by bassist Ray Brown and vibraphonist Milt Jackson, released on Verve (V6-8615). Tracks include "Lined With a Groove", "For Someone I Love", "Dew and Mud" and "Lazy Theme".

before: Austin Richard Post, known professionally as Post Malone, is an American singer, rapper, songwriter, record producer, and actor. His music blends various genres including pop, hip-hop, trap, country, R&B, and rock.
after: Russell Malone is the self-titled 1992 album by jazz guitarist Russell Malone, released on Columbia (52825). Tracks include "Wives and Lovers", "When I Take My Sugar to Tea", "St. Louis Blues" and "Moonlight Serenade".

before: Louis Daniel Armstrong, nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist. He was among the most influential figures in jazz. His career spanned five decades and several eras in the history of jazz. Armstrong received numerous accolades including the Grammy Award for Best Male Vocal Performance for Hello, Dolly! in 1965, as well as a posthumous win for the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award in 1972.
after: Satchmo is a 1987 compilation of trumpeter and vocalist Louis Armstrong, released on Pickwick Compact Discs (PWK 009). Tracks include "Back o' Town Blues", "Tiger Rag", "St. James Infirmary" and "When the Saints Go Marching In".

before: Pool is an album by John Zorn featuring his early "game piece" composition of the same name which was first released on vinyl on Parachute Records in 1980 as a double album including the composition "Hockey". The album was released on CD on Tzadik Records with an additional bonus track featuring a test recording of Archery as part of The Parachute Years Box Set in 1997 and as a single CD in 2000. The album was the first released solely under Zorn's name following his collaboration with Eugene Chadbourne, School (1978). Rolling Stone noted that "[alt]hough Zorn has operated almost entirely outside the mainstream, he's gradually asserted himself as one of the most influential musicians of our time".
after: School is a 1978 album by Eugene Chadbourne and John Zorn, released on Parachute Records (P004/6). Tracks include "Solitude", "Duet", "The Return of Romance", "The Shreeve" and "The Fling".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title School, artists Eugene Chadbourne + John Zorn, date 1978, label Parachute P004/6, tracklist

before: "Singing the Blues" is a popular song composed by Melvin Endsley and published in 1956. The highest-charting version was by Guy Mitchell and the first recording of the song was by Marty Robbins. It is not related to the 1920 jazz song "Singin' the Blues" recorded by Frank Trumbauer and Bix Beiderbecke in 1927. Beiderbecke was one of the most influential jazz soloists of the 1920s, a cornet player noted for an inventive lyrical approach and purity of tone, with such clarity of sound that one contemporary famously described it like "shooting bullets at a bel.
after: Singin' the Blues is a compilation of cornetist Bix Beiderbecke, released on Drive Archive in 1995. It gathers his 1920s recordings, including the title track "Singin' the Blues", "I'm Comin' Virginia", "Riverboat Shuffle" and "Ostrich Walk".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes, sim 1.0): title Singin' the Blues, artist Bix Beiderbecke, firstReleaseDate 1995, label Drive Archive, secondaryType Compilation, tracklist

before: Trilogy is a 2013 live album by Chick Corea and his trio, featuring Corea on piano with Christian McBride on double bass and Brian Blade on drums. The three-disc album was recorded live in Washington, D.C., and Oakland, California, and in Spain, Switzerland, Austria, Slovenia, Turkey, and Japan. The album peaked at number nine on the Billboard Jazz Albums chart in 2014. It won the 2014 Grammy Award for Best Jazz Instrumental Album.
after: Solo Piano - Portraits is a 2014 solo-piano album by Chick Corea, released on Concord Jazz. Corea plays standards, originals and improvised portraits, including "Improv #1 / How Deep Is the Ocean?", "Waltz for Debby" and "Blue Monk".
